heading: CHE PURIFICA E PACIFICA TUTTE LE COSE
card_summary: This chunk opens the third "Tono" of the second "Cantico" of Zorzi's De Harmonia Mundi, treating the figure of the priest as mediator between God and humanity, with Christ as the supreme fulfillment of the priestly office. Zorzi enumerates seven priestly functions drawn from Leviticus, Deuteronomy, Malachi, Hebrews, and James — intercession, sacrifice, teaching, military leadership, guidance of the people, judicial authority, and pastoral care — demonstrating that Christ perfectly fulfilled each. He then argues that all priestly prerogatives of the Mosaic law (firstfruits, called terumah in Hebrew; guilt-offerings called asham; vestments) are typologically transferred to Christ. The section further develops a detailed philological and typological discussion of the eight sacerdotal vestments (miknese, ketonet, avnet, miznephet, and the high-priestly garments) with Hebrew terminology drawn from Josephus, Jerome, and Origen. Note: this summary covers the opening portion of a very long section (48,237 words).
essay:
# CHE PURIFICA E PACIFICA TUTTE LE COSE — Scholarly Summary

*Note: This summary is based on the opening portion (approximately the first 6,000 words, corresponding to Chapters 1–4 of the Tono Terzo, Cantico Secondo) of a very long section running from pp. 1395 to 1517. The remainder of the section, which continues for tens of thousands of additional words, has not been read in full.*

---

## Overview and Context

This chunk opens the third "Tono" (tone/movement) of the "Cantico Secondo" in Zorzi's *De Harmonia Mundi*. The overarching title — "Che purifica e pacifica tutte le cose" ("He who purifies and pacifies all things") — announces the section's Christological theme: Christ as the cosmic high priest whose sacrificial mediation restores the harmony shattered by sin. The section belongs to Zorzi's broader project of demonstrating that the entire Levitical and prophetic tradition of Israel is a typological pre-figuration of the cosmic Mediator, and that the priestly order — from its Mosaic institutions to its evangelical fulfillment — encodes the same triadic structure (God, mediator, people) that underlies the harmonic architecture of creation.

---

## Chapter 1: The Nature and Ministry of the Priest (Cap. I)

Zorzi opens with a definition of the priest as *terminus medius inter Deum et hominem*, a visible God on earth. He then enumerates **seven priestly functions**, each grounded in specific scriptural loci:

1. **Intercession** (*oratio pro populo*): Leviticus, twice, with the Hebrew phrase *we-kipper 'alaw ha-kohen* (וְכִפֶּר עָלָיו הַכֹּהֵן) — "the priest will make atonement for him" — and James 5 on the anointing of the sick.
2. **Sacrifice** (*oblatio hostiarum*): Hebrews 5 and 7, which requires that every high priest offer gifts and victims.
3. **Teaching** (*doctrina populi*): Aggai and Malachi — the priest as "angel of the Lord of hosts," guardian of the law.
4. **Military leadership** (*dux divini exercitus*): Deuteronomy 20, with the priest encouraging the people before battle. Zorzi illustrates this with the episode of Phinehas (Finees) commanding twelve thousand Israelites against Midian (Numbers 31), with Moses and Aaron holding up their hands in prayer during the battle against Amalek, and with post-scriptural exempla: Martin of Tours facing enemy squadrons under the banner of Christ; Pope Leo I halting Attila, whose army saw two sword-bearing figures (identified as Peter and Paul, or possibly angels) flanking the pope; Elisha at Dothan (2 Kings 6) surrounded by Assyrians, with the servant's eyes opened to see horses and chariots of fire; and Giovanni da Capistrano at the siege of Belgrade.
5. **Guidance to the promised land** (*ducere populum ad finem*): Joshua leading the Israelites across the Jordan, interpreted typologically as baptism — the priest divides the waters, with one part remaining sweet (those who fulfil their baptismal promises) and one part turning bitter (those who corrupt grace through the vices of flesh and world).
6. **Judicial authority** (*iudicium*): Deuteronomy 17, directing difficult cases to the Levitical priests. Zorzi inserts a sharp critique of contemporary clergy who are elected to the priesthood without knowledge of the law, "la feccia dell'ignoranza e le cloache della turpitudine."
7. **Pastoral care** (*cura animarum*): the good shepherd of John 10, who gives his life for his sheep.

> "il sacerdote deve condurre il popolo al fine per esso stabilito, come si legge che avvenne nel libro di Giosuè in modo tipologico: quando gli israeliti attraversavano il Giordano per raggiungere la terra promessa, i sacerdoti precedevano il popolo dividendo le acque del Giordano."
> <cite>p. 1231</cite>

---

## Chapter 2: Christ as the True Priest (Cap. II)

Zorzi systematically applies each of the seven priestly functions to Christ, demonstrating his fulfilment of the Levitical type. The chapter moves through the Gospel and Pauline evidence:

- **Intercession**: Christ's prayer in Gethsemane (John 17), his prayer for Peter (Luke 22), and his present intercession at the Father's right hand (Hebrews 7:25).
- **Sacrifice**: the self-offering of the Lamb, once for all — Hebrews 10:12 (*Hic autem unam pro peccatis offerens hostiam, in sempiternum sedet ad dexteram Dei*), 1 Peter 3:18, Ephesians 5:2.
- **Teaching**: the doctrine hidden from the wise of the world, received from the *Pater luminum* (James 1:17) — the child Jesus astonishing the doctors (Luke 2), with the note that "in lui sono riposti tutti i tesori della sapienza e della scienza" (Colossians 2:3).
- **Military leadership**: the defeat of Satan in the desert, then on behalf of all humanity; Colossians 2:15 (*expolians principatus et potestates*); 1 Corinthians 15:57.
- **Leadership of the nations**: Micah 5:2 as cited in Matthew 2:6 (*ex te enim egredietur dux qui regat populum meum Israel*), and Isaiah 55:3–4 on the eternal covenant, the "faithful mercies of David" — Zorzi interprets "David" throughout as a figure for Christ.
- **Pastoral sacrifice**: Christ presenting the lost sheep (human nature) on his shoulders to the Father who dwells in inaccessible light (1 Timothy 6:16).

> "Cristo GESÙ, consacrato re, profeta e sacerdote davanti a tutte le sue schiere, fu a buon diritto Re dei re, Profeta dei profeti, Sacerdote dei sacerdoti ovvero Pontefice dei pontefici."
> <cite>p. 1233</cite>

---

## Chapter 3: All Priestly Prerogatives Are Owed to Christ (Cap. III)

This chapter is the most technically dense in the opening section. Zorzi turns to the specific ritual allocations of the Mosaic law — firstfruits, tithes, guilt-offerings — and argues that their transfer to Christ is both exegetically necessary and numerologically significant.

**Terumah and terumah de terumah**: Zorzi introduces the Hebrew technical term *terumah* (תְּרוּמָה, "heave-offering" or "firstfruits"), citing Numbers 18. He follows Origen in rejecting the literal application of this law (since the physical offerings were consumed and passed away) and insists they must be referred to the *lex spiritus*. The Pauline "firstfruits of the spirit" (Romans 8:23) are identified as either the charismatic gifts of the primitive church or the "buone ispirazioni" — the good impulses that must be consecrated to Christ the high priest.

**Numerical symbolism**: In a characteristic Zorzian move combining Kabbalistic gematria with Neoplatonic number theory, he notes that the people gave 1-in-10 to the priests (*decem*), and the priests gave 1-in-10 of that to God (*unum*), while the people as a whole received the remainder (*centum*). God as the simplest being receives the one; the material people receive the hundred; the priest as mediator occupies the ten, "che è la fine dei numeri e il principio delle decine nonché il medio tra uno e cento." Christ the God-man is owed *both* the one (as God) and the ten (as human priest).

**Asham** (אָשָׁם): Zorzi identifies the guilt-offering for sins committed through ignorance, citing Leviticus 5 with the Hebrew text and transliteration. He follows Origen in distinguishing sins of commission (*peccato commissionis*) from sins of omission (*peccato omissionis*), while the literal sense refers to sin committed in error (*per errorem*). The priest's assumption of the guilt-offering typologically pre-figures Christ taking on the iniquities of all (Isaiah 53; Hebrews 9:28; Hebrews 10:10; Ephesians 5:2).

**Confession and absolution**: The breast and right thigh (*pectusculum* and *armus*) owed to the priest in Leviticus are interpreted allegorically: the breast harbours the heart (thoughts), while *armus* in Hebrew (יָרֵך, *yarek*) signifies both "thigh" and "concupiscence." These must be placed in the hands of a "buon sacerdote e saggio" in confession, so he may judge and purify them.

**The Lord's Day and the octave**: In a digression on liturgical time, Zorzi argues that whereas the Mosaic law sanctified the seventh day, Christians give Christ the *first* day — the Lord's Day (*dies dominica*) — because the resurrection on that day inaugurated eternal life. Some interpreters hold that the Last Day will also fall on a Sunday, "come in un diapason perfetto a conclusione di molti cicli di ottave." David's octave-psalms are cited in this context. This passage exemplifies the harmonic cosmology running throughout the *De Harmonia Mundi*: temporal liturgy as musical structure.

> "A Dio, dunque, che è l'Essere semplicissimo andava l'uno, al popolo materiale il cento, mentre al sacerdote, che occupa una posizione intermedia tra Dio e il popolo, andava il dieci, che è la fine dei numeri e il principio delle decine nonché il medio tra uno e cento. A Cristo, invece, nostro sommo Sacerdote, spettano l'uno e il dieci."
> <cite>p. 1239</cite>

---

## Chapter 4: The Priestly Vestments (Cap. IV)

The section concludes its opening movement with a learned catalogue of the eight priestly garments, drawing on Josephus (*Antiquitates Iudaicae*), Jerome, and implied Kabbalistic philology. Zorzi enumerates:

1. **Miknese** (מִכְנְסֵי, miknese) — linen breeches, Greek ἀναξυρίδες, Latin *foeminalia* or *bracae*; woven of twisted byssus (Josephus).
2. **Ketonet** (כֻּתֹּנֶת) — the tunic, Greek χιτών, Latin *tunica* or *camisia*.
3. **Avnet** (אַבְנֵט) — the girdle or *cingulum*; Zorzi describes it as resembling "a serpent skin" shedding old age, woven with scarlet, purple, hyacinth, and byssus in a *polymita* (multicoloured) technique so artful that Jerome said the flowers and gems seemed added rather than woven.
4. **Miznephet** (מִצְנֶפֶת) — the round turban, Greek τιάρα, covering most of the head but leaving a third of the forehead exposed, fastened at the back.

These four are common to all priests. Four additional vestments belong exclusively to the high priest, beginning with the **Maeil** (מְעִיל) — the all-blue tunic with 72 bells (*tintinnabula*) and 72 pomegranates (*malagranata*) alternating at the hem, so that the high priest would "make a sound" (*totus vocalis*) entering the Temple, lest he die.

> "In quarto luogo egli è costituito comandante dell'esercito divino, per guidare ed esortare il popolo più debole e sostenere per primo l'assalto dei nemici... il sacerdote si accosterà al popolo e vi dirà: Non tema il vostro cuore, non cedete, non tremate davanti a loro, perché il Signore vostro Dio marcia con voi."
> <cite>p. 1231</cite>

---

## Key Sources and Technical Vocabulary

- **Scripture**: Leviticus (passim), Numbers 18, Deuteronomy 17 and 20, Joshua, Aggai, Malachi, Isaiah 55, Micah 5, 1 Peter 3, Hebrews 5, 7, 9, 10, Romans 8, Colossians 2, Ephesians 5, James 5, Luke 2 and 22, John 10 and 17.
- **Patristic**: Origen (on firstfruits as spiritual goods; on the *asham*; on Alexander the Great and the Jewish high priest, from *Contra Celsum*); Jerome (on the girdle).
- **Historical/hagiographical**: Josephus (*Antiquitates Iudaicae*) on priestly vestments; Leo I and Attila; Martin of Tours; Giovanni da Capistrano at Belgrade.
- **Hebrew terms introduced**: *we-kipper 'alaw ha-kohen* (Leviticus atonement formula); *terumah* (firstfruits/heave-offering); *terumah de-terumah* (firstfruits of firstfruits); *asham* (guilt offering); *miknese*, *ketonet*, *avnet*, *miznephet*, *maeil*, *yarek* (concupiscence/thigh); the number seventy-two in the bells and pomegranates of the *maeil*.
- **Cosmological framework**: The medial position of the priest (ten, between one and hundred) and the priestly vestments are treated as encoded harmonic ratios, consistent with Zorzi's larger project of reading the Mosaic institutions as expressions of cosmic musical proportionality. The Sunday/octave digression explicitly invokes the *diapason* as the structural model for liturgical time.
